To make it: dig a pit or use a metal drum, fill with dry crop residues, wood chips, or husks, ignite and let it burn slowly with limited air (not fully exposed to oxygen), cover when partially charred to stop burning, let it cool, crush into small pieces, mix with compost before adding to soil. Biochar lasts in soil for hundreds of years and improves water and nutrient retention.
response_tw: Biochar y…õ efi a w…îy…õ fi nhwiren foforo a …õsiesie asase bere a w…îde ka ho. Y…õ no eyi: hu tumtum anaa fa metal drum, hye nnuaba nsennua a ayaw, dua bi anaa nkuto mu, hyew na ma …õhye br…õ br…õ a mframa ketewa w…î mu (amfa oxygen mu p…õp…õ…õp…õ), s…õ…õ bere a …õrehye kakra s…õ …õnhye bio, ma …õhuru, tutu y…õ mmer…õ, ka compost ho ansa na wode ka asase ho. Biochar tena asase mu mfe ahabiri na …õsiesie nsuo ne aduan guina.

instruction_en: What is vermicomposting and how do I start it?
instruction_tw: D…õn na vermicomposting y…õ na …õde…õn na mesi ase?
response_en: Vermicomposting uses earthworms to break down organic waste into very high-quality compost called vermicompost or worm castings. To start: get a container or build a wooden box, add bedding of moist shredded newspaper or cardboard, add earthworms (red wigglers are best), feed with fruit and vegetable scraps, coffee grounds, and eggshells. Avoid meat, dairy, and oily food. Harvest the rich compost from the bottom every 2-3 months.
response_tw: Vermicomposting fa asase nwir…õntwi di dwuma s…õ p…õ nhwiren foforo asennua y…õ compost a …õw…î aduan pa pa ara a w…îfre vermicompost anaa worm castings. S…õ wohwehw…õ si ase: nya nkuku anaa y…õ dua box, de krataa anaa cardboard a w…îatwa twa na w…îapo mu de y…õ mpa, de asase nwir…õntwi hy…õ (red wiggler y…õ pa pa ara), de nnuan ne aba asennua, coffee, ne kosua nkuku hy…õ. B…î hw…õ nam, nkyene aduan, ne ngo aduan. Gye compost a …õw…î ase h…î no abosome 2-3 biara.

instruction_en: How do I use natural methods to control pests in my vegetable garden?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na metumi kora adwummakers ase nhwiren ho w…î nnuan afuo mu?
response_en: Organic pest control methods for vegetables include: handpicking large insects and caterpillars, using physical barriers like row covers or netting, companion planting with pest-repelling plants, spraying with homemade neem oil, garlic, or chili solutions, attracting beneficial insects with flowering plants, using kaolin clay sprays to confuse insects, and maintaining healthy soil and plants as the best long-term defense.
response_tw: Nhwiren adwummakers kora anamm…în ma nnuan ne: twe adwummakers k…õse ne abor…înoma w…î nsa, fa akwantempon a w…îde nnuaba so te s…õ row covers anaa net, ka nnuaba a w…îkora adwummakers ase ho, guare fie neem dawuro, gyene, anaa mako a w…îay…õ, twe adwummakers pa w…î abode nnuaba ho, fa kaolin clay guare s…õ …õbue adwummakers no, na kora asase ne nnuaba aho yie s…õ anamm…în pa ara w…î bere tenten mu.

instruction_en: What is the push-pull farming system?
instruction_tw: D…õn na push-pull farming syst…õm y…õ?
response_en: Push-pull is a clever intercropping system developed in Africa that controls stemborers (the most destructive maize pest) without chemicals. Plant Desmodium grass between maize rows (it repels/pushes stemborers away) and Napier grass or Brachiaria around the field border (it attracts/pulls stemborers and traps them). Desmodium also fixes nitrogen and suppresses Striga weed. This system is free to maintain once established.
response_tw: Push-pull y…õ intercropping anamm…în ahorow a w…îy…õ w…î Afrika a …õkora stemborer (aburow adwummaker a …õs…õe pa ara) ase a w…îmfa chemicals. To Desmodium at…õnt…õnni w…î aburow nnua ntam (…õkora/push stemborer k…î) na Napier at…õnt…õnni anaa Brachiaria twa afuo no ho (…õtwe/pull stemborer na gye w…în). Desmodium nso fi nitrogen na kora Striga nhaban foforo ase. Anamm…în no y…õ daa akyi a w…îasi no yie.

instruction_en: Can I grow crops without monitoring them constantly?
instruction_tw: Metumi kura nnuaba a menhw…õ ho saa ara anaa?
response_en: Growing without any external inputs is very difficult and usually leads to low yields. However, you can minimize purchased inputs by: making your own compost and manure, saving your own open-pollinated seeds, making homemade organic pesticides, using crop rotation to naturally restore soil fertility, and relying on rainwater. This approach requires more knowledge and management but can be cost-effective for subsistence farming.
response_tw: Kura a wonhw…õ ho adenne…õ biara y…õ den pa ara na taa ma nnuaba da sor sua. Nanso wobetumi sua adenne…õ a wot…î w…î: y…õ wo compost ne aguare ho, gye wo open-pollinated aba, y…õ fie nhwiren adwummakers dawuro, fa nnuaba sesae s…õ siesie asase ho nhwiren a enni sika, na fa osu nsuo. Anamm…în no hia nyansam ne hw…õ ase dodo nanso tumi y…õ ade…õ pa ma fie okuafo adwuma.

instruction_en: What can I do so my harvested produce does not go to waste?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na metumi y…õ na m'aduan a megye no akyi anko mpoano?
response_en: Harvest at the right time, handle produce gently to avoid bruising, dry grains properly, store in appropriate conditions, transport in ventilated containers, and sell quickly for perishables. Post-harvest losses in Ghana can reach 30-40%.
response_tw: Gye nnuaba bere a …õs…õ, de nnuaba no a eye yie s…õ …õnhow, yaw nnuaba a ayaw no yie, guina w…î bae a …õs…õ, de k…î bae a mframa tumi pue mu, na t…în nt…õm s…õ …õy…õ aduan a …õs…õe nt…õm. Ghana mu aduan a …õko ase…õ w…î gye akyi betumi k…î 30-40%.

instruction_en: How do I properly dry my produce after harvesting?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na meyaw m'nnuaba yie akyi a megye w…în?
response_en: Spread grains on clean tarpaulins or raised drying platforms in the sun. Stir regularly for even drying. Avoid drying on bare soil which introduces dirt and moisture. Grains are dry enough when a handful feels cool not warm and hard seeds do not dent easily.
response_tw: To nnuaba w…î tarpaulin horo anaa platform a …õda sor w…î owia mu. Sane w…în p…õ s…õ w…îyaw p…õ. B…î hw…õ yaw w…î asase firi so efis…õ …õde fufuo ne nsuo ba mu. Nnuaba yaw yie bere a wo nsa a wode fa no y…õ huru na aba den nyi w…î nsateaa ase nt…õm.

instruction_en: My harvested tomatoes are rotting quickly. What can I do?
instruction_tw: M'ntomato a megye w…în res…õe nt…õm. D…õn na metumi y…õ?
response_en: Avoid harvesting during wet conditions. Handle gently to prevent bruising. Store in cool shaded well-ventilated areas on raised surfaces. Remove and separate any rotting fruits immediately. Consider making tomato paste or drying to reduce losses. Transport in ventilated crates not plastic bags.
response_tw: B…î hw…õ gye bere a nsuo w…î h…î. De w…în p…õ s…õ w…înhow. Guina w…î bae a …õhuru, owia ntra mu, mframa pue mu na …õda sor. Yi na twa ntomato a res…õe nt…õm ara. Fa tomato paste y…õ anaa yaw s…õ mpampa sika k…î mpoano.

instruction_en: How do I reduce post-harvest losses for vegetables?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na metumi sua nnuan a megye w…în akyi mpoano?
response_en: Harvest vegetables early in the morning when cool, handle gently, sort out damaged ones immediately, store in cool ventilated areas, and sell quickly. For leafy vegetables, wrap in damp cloth to extend freshness. Avoid long transport in hot conditions.
response_tw: Gye nnuan owia da ano bere a …õhuru, de w…în p…õ, yi a w…îas…õe nt…õm ara, guina w…î bae a …õhuru na mframa pue mu, na t…în nt…õm. Nhaban nnuan ho, de ntama nte de s…õ…õ w…în s…õ …õma w…în tena p…õ.

instruction_en: What is grading and why is it important when selling produce?
instruction_tw: D…õn na grading y…õ na …õs…õ s…õ …õy…õ papa s…õ wot…în nnuaba?
response_en: Grading means sorting produce into quality categories based on size, color, shape, and freedom from defects. It is important because: buyers pay premium prices for Grade A produce, graded produce has longer shelf life when sorted, it builds trust with buyers and processors, it allows you to sell different grades to different markets, and it makes it easier to negotiate better prices. Grade A goes to supermarkets, Grade B to restaurants, Grade C to processors.
response_tw: Grading kyer…õ yi nnuaba no mu w…î aba ho pa ahodo…î w…î bo…î, rere, ahof√£, ne mfomso a enni ho so. ∆êho hia efis…õ: ade…õt…îfo tua sika dodo ma Grade A nnuaba, nnuaba a w…îasi no mu tena yie bere tenten, …õkyer…õ ade…õt…îfo ne adwuma kuo s…õ wobetumi de w…în so, …õma wo tumi t…în Grade ahodo…î w…î aguabasa ahodo…î, na …õboa wo ne ade…õt…îfo siesie bo…î yie. Grade A k…î supermarkets, Grade B k…î adidibea, Grade C k…î adwuma kuo.

instruction_en: How do I process vegetables so they last longer?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mey…õ nnuan a ayaw s…õ w…îtena yie bere tenten?
response_en: Dry vegetables by: washing and slicing thinly, blanching (briefly boiling) leafy vegetables for 1-2 minutes before drying to preserve color and nutrients, spreading on clean raised drying racks in the sun, or using a solar dryer. Vegetables are dry when crisp and hard ‚Äî usually 2-5 days in sun. Pack in sealed airtight bags and store in a cool dry place. Dried vegetables last 6-12 months and can be sold as a higher-value product.
response_tw: Yaw nnuan w…î: hohoro na twa twa yie, blanch (hyew kakra) nhaban nnuan s…õnt…î 1-2 ansa na woyaw s…õ …õkora w…în rere ne aduan ho nhwiren, de to drying rack horo a …õda sor w…î owia mu, anaa fa solar dryer. Nnuan yaw wie bere a w…îy…õ den na w…îpae ‚Äî taa nnafoa 2-5 w…î owia mu. Paka w…î bags a apo so na guina w…î bae a …õhuru ne yera. Nnuan a ayaw tena abosome 6-12 na wobetumi t…în w…î bo…î k…õse.

instruction_en: How do I make peanut butter from groundnuts?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mey…õ nkatie betere fi nkatie mu?
response_en: To make peanut butter: sort and remove damaged nuts, roast in a pan on low heat until golden brown (20-30 minutes), stir constantly to prevent burning, remove skins while warm, grind in a blender or milling machine until smooth, add salt to taste, and pack in clean sealed containers. Can add a little vegetable oil if too thick. Homemade peanut butter sells well and has a 2-3 month shelf life at room temperature.
response_tw: Y…õ nkatie betere eyi: yi na si nnuaba a as…õe, hyew w…î pan so ogya sua mu kosi s…õ …õdan hoho b…î…î (s…õnt…î 20-30), sane p…õ s…õ …õnhyew, yi nkuto a …õw…î so bere a …õhuru, blend w…î blender anaa mill mu kosi s…õ …õy…õ mmer…õ, de …õwa ka ho s…õ d…õm, na paka w…î nkuku horo a apo so. Tumi de vegetable dawuro kakra ka ho s…õ …õhwa pa dodo. Fie nkatie betere t…în yie na …õtena abosome 2-3 w…î fie osu mu.

instruction_en: How do I make oil at home?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mey…õ ngo w…î fie?
response_en: To process palm oil at home: harvest fresh bunches and ferment for 3-5 days to loosen fruits, thresh fruits from the bunch, boil fruits for 30-60 minutes until soft, pound or press to separate oil from fiber, add water and boil the pulp again, skim oil from the surface, filter through cloth, boil the filtered oil to remove water (until bubbling stops), and cool before storing in sealed containers. Traditional palm press improves extraction efficiency.
response_tw: Y…õ ngo w…î fie eyi: gye b…î foforo na ma w…în da nnafoa 3-5 s…õ aba no pae yie, gye aba fi b…î no ho, hyew aba no s…õnt…î 30-60 kosi s…õ w…îy…õ mmer…õ, tutu anaa p…õm s…õ yi ngo fi nsennua mu, de nsuo ka aba no ka ho na hyew bio, yi ngo fi so, hun w…î ntama mu, hyew ngo a w…îahun no s…õ yi nsuo (kosi s…õ bubble ahwie), na ma …õhuru ansa na waguina w…î nkuku a apo so. Traditional palm press boa s…õ wunya ngo dodo.

instruction_en: How do I add value to cassava to earn more money?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mede bo…î ka bankye ho s…õ menya sika dodo?
response_en: Cassava value-added products include: gari (fermented and roasted cassava), fufu flour, cassava flour for baking, cassava chips (dried slices), cassava starch for industrial use, kokonte (dried cassava), and high-quality fufu. Each step of processing adds value and income. Gari is especially popular and can double the income from fresh cassava. Consider forming a women's group to process together to reduce costs and increase volumes.
response_tw: Bankye ho adwuma a w…îde y…õ dodo ne: gari (bankye a w…îade na hyew), fufu mfurow, bankye mfurow ma bobe adwuma, bankye chips (slices a ayaw), bankye starch ma adwuma kuro, kokonte (bankye a ayaw), ne fufu a …õw…î aho pa. Adwuma biara a w…îde y…õ de bo…î ne sika ka ho. Gari p…õ pa ara na …õtumi ma bankye foforo sika abien. Hw…õ si mmaa kuo s…õ w…îy…õ adwuma bom s…õ sua sika ne ma nnuaba dodo ba.

instruction_en: How do I grow pepper successfully?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mekura mako yie?
response_en: Pepper grows best in warm sunny conditions with well-drained fertile soil. Start seeds in a nursery then transplant 4-6 week old seedlings. Space 45-60 cm apart. Water regularly, stake tall plants, and apply balanced fertilizer.
response_tw: Mako da sor pa w…î owia mu na asase a nsuo pue mu yie na …õw…î aduan pa. Fi aba fi nursery, afei si nnuaba a w…îdin nnawotwe 4-6 w…î w…î beae fofor…î. Gyae asukyer…õ s…õntimita 45-60. De nsuo to so p…õ, si aduro nnua a w…îreden, na de ferefere p…õ to so.

instruction_en: How long does it take for cowpea to mature?
instruction_tw: Bere ahe na …õs…õ ma ab…îdwe…õ awie ase?
response_en: Cowpea matures in 60-90 days depending on the variety. Harvest when pods turn yellow-brown and dry. For fresh green beans, harvest when pods are full but still green about 60 days after planting.
response_tw: Ab…îdwe…õ wie ase w…î nnafoa 60-90 mu w…î aba no ahodo…î so. Gye bere a fidie no dan sika …õda na ayera. Ab…îdwe…õ foforo ho, gye bere a fidie no ay…õ ma nanso …õda koraa nnafoa 60 akyi a wode to mu.

instruction_en: How do I grow onions in the dry season?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mekura gyene w…î …îp…õ bere mu?
response_en: Onions grow best in cool dry conditions. Plant sets or transplants in the dry season November to February in northern Ghana. Irrigate regularly. Harvest when tops fall over naturally. Dry in shade before storage.
response_tw: Gyene da sor pa w…î huru tirim na …õyera. To sets anaa transplants w…î …îp…õ bere mu. De nsuo to so p…õ. Gye bere a w…în tenten atu. Yaw w…î owia sum ansa na waguina.

instruction_en: How do I grow watermelon successfully?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mekura watermelon yie?
response_en: Watermelon needs well-drained sandy loam soil, warm temperatures, and plenty of sunlight. Space 1.5-2 m apart. Water deeply but infrequently. Apply potassium-rich fertilizer during fruiting. Harvest when the tendril near the fruit turns brown.
response_tw: Watermelon hia asinti…õ asase a nsuo pue mu yie, owia mu, na owia hye pa. Gyae asukyer…õ mita 1.5-2. De nsuo to asase mu yiye nanso mmere pii bi. De potassium ferefere to so bere a aba reba. Gye bere a ntini a …õb…õn aba no dan …õda.

instruction_en: How do I increase my cocoa yield?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na metumi ma m'kookoo da sor dodo?
response_en: Prune cocoa trees regularly to remove dead wood and improve light. Apply recommended fertilizers. Control black pod disease by removing infected pods and spraying copper-based fungicides. Replace unproductive old trees with improved varieties.
response_tw: Twa kookoo nnua so p…õ s…õ yi dua kum ne nnua a w…îawu fi mu na ma owia hy…õ mu. De ferefere a w…îaka to so. Kora black pod yade…õ ase w…î yi pods a w…îayar…õ na de copper fungicide guare so. Si nnua kedee a wonnya bi fi w…în ho na de aba fofor…î a …õda sor w…î w…în beae.

instruction_en: What causes cocoa black pod disease and how do I control it?
instruction_tw: D…õn na …õma kookoo black pod yade…õ na …õde…õn na metumi kora ase?
response_en: Black pod is caused by Phytophthora fungus and spreads in wet humid conditions. It turns pods black and destroys beans. Control by removing and burying infected pods, pruning trees for better air circulation, and spraying copper-based fungicides regularly during rainy season.
response_tw: Black pod fi Phytophthora fungus na …õtete w…î nsuo ne huhu mu. ∆êdan pods tuntum na …õs…õe aba no. Kora ase w…î yi ne sisie pods a w…îayar…õ, twa nnua so s…õ mframa pue mu yie, na de copper fungicide guare p…õ w…î osu bere mu.

instruction_en: How do I grow cucumber successfully?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mekura kakaduro yie?
response_en: Ginger grows best in partially shaded humid conditions with rich well-drained soil. Plant rhizomes 5-7 cm deep spaced 20-30 cm apart. Keep soil moist and mulch heavily. Harvest after 8-10 months when leaves yellow and die back.
response_tw: Kakaduro da sor pa w…î beae a owia tra mu kakra na …õw…î huhu, asase a …õw…î aduan pa na nsuo pue mu yie. De rhizomes to asase mu s…õntimita 5-7 na gyae asukyer…õ s…õntimita 20-30. Kora asase nte na de mulch to so papaapa. Gye abosome 8-10 akyi a nhaban no dan sika na awu.

instruction_en: When should I harvest rice?
instruction_tw: Bere b…õn na …õs…õ s…õ megye …ît…õ?
response_en: Harvest sorghum when grain is hard, heads have turned their mature color, and moisture content is around 20-25%. Dry after harvest before storage. Harvest promptly to avoid bird damage.
response_tw: Gye …ît…õ bere a aba no ay…õ den, aba no dinn adan w…în rere na nsukuro y…õ b…õn 20-25%. Yaw akyi a wogye ansa na waguina. Gye nt…õm s…õ anomaa mfomso anba.

instruction_en: What is the best fertilizer for plantain?
instruction_tw: Ferefere b…õn na …õy…õ …îkor…î ma bor…îd…î?
response_en: Plantain responds well to organic matter such as manure and compost plus potassium-rich fertilizers. Apply NPK 15-15-15 at planting then top-dress with muriate of potash during bunch development. Potassium is critical for good bunch weight and disease resistance.
response_tw: Bor…îd…î gye nhwiren foforo aduan pa te s…õ aguare ne compost a potassium ferefere ka ho yiye. De NPK 15-15-15 to mu bere a wode to mu afei de muriate of potash to so bere a aba reba. Potassium ho hia pa ara ma b…î k…õse ne yade…õ tumi.

instruction_en: How do I control Panama disease in plantain and banana?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na metumi kora Panama yade…õ ase w…î bor…îd…î ne kwadu mu?
response_en: Panama disease is a fatal fungal soil disease. Use certified disease-free planting material, avoid planting in previously infected soil, practice strict hygiene by cleaning tools before moving between farms, and plant resistant varieties like Cardaba or FHIA hybrids. There is no chemical cure.
response_tw: Panama yade…õ y…õ fungal asase yade…õ a …õkum. De aba a w…îakyer…õw w…î ho a enni yade…õ to mu, b…î hw…õ to w…î asase a yade…õ aka ho ansa, y…õ ho tumi yie w…î hohoro ade…õ ansa na wosesa afuo, na to aba a …õtumi hy…õ ase te s…õ Cardaba anaa FHIA.

instruction_en: How do I grow plantain successfully?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mekura bor…îd…î yie?
response_en: Plant plantain suckers from healthy mother plants. Choose planting material free from pests. Dig holes 60cm x 60cm x 60cm and fill with compost. Space plants 3m x 3m. Mulch heavily, water in dry periods, and apply potassium-rich fertilizer for good bunch development.
response_tw: To bor…îd…î aba fi nnua a w…îw…î aho na w…îsom yie. Paw aba a enni adwummakers. Hu tumtum s…õntimita 60 x 60 x 60 na hye compost mu. Gyae asukyer…õ mita 3 x 3. De mulch to so papaapa, de nsuo to …îp…õ bere mu, na de potassium ferefere to s…õ aba reba yie.

instruction_en: Which rice varieties perform best in Ghana?
instruction_tw: ∆Üm…î aba b…õn na …õda sor pa w…î Ghana?
response_en: Popular rice varieties in Ghana include AGRA Rice, Jasmine 85, and NERICA varieties. NERICA varieties are resistant to drought and local pests. Contact your local MOFA extension officer for variety recommendations suited to your specific area.
response_tw: ∆Üm…î aba a w…îp…õ w…î Ghana ho ne AGRA Rice, Jasmine 85, ne NERICA aba. NERICA aba tumi nsuo pa ne beae adwummakers ase hy…õ. Ka kyer…õ wo MOFA extension officer a …õb…õn wo s…õ …îka aba a …õfata wo beae ho.

instruction_en: How do I grow cashew successfully?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mekura cashew yie?
response_en: Cashew grows best in well-drained sandy or laterite soils in areas with a distinct dry season. Plant seeds directly 5 cm deep in the field or transplant seedlings. Space trees 8-10 meters apart. Apply fertilizer in the second year onward. Control collar rot disease, cashew stem and root borer insects, and anthracnose. Harvest nuts when the cashew apple turns yellow or red and the nut is dark grey. Cashew trees start producing at 3-5 years.
response_tw: Cashew da sor pa w…î asase a nsuo pue mu yie a …õy…õ asinti…õ anaa laterite w…î beae a …îp…õ bere w…î h…î. To aba t…ît…î w…î asase mu s…õntimita 5 anaa si nnuaba mfoforo ananmu. Gyae nnua asukyer…õ mita 8-10. De ferefere to mfe a …õto so abien ananmu. Kora collar rot yade…õ, cashew tibo ne ntini adwummakers, ne anthracnose ase. Gye aba bere a cashew apple no dan hoho anaa k…îk…î…î na aba no dan tuntum-br…îdwa. Cashew nnua da sor fi mfe 3-5 akyi.

instruction_en: How do I grow soybean commercially to make profit?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mekura soya y…õ adwuma s…õ menya sika?
response_en: Soybeans are highly profitable legumes. Plant after rains are well established. Inoculate seeds with Bradyrhizobium bacteria before planting (this fixes nitrogen and can triple yields). Space 30-45 cm within rows and 60 cm between rows. Apply phosphorus fertilizer at planting. Weed once at 3 weeks. Harvest when 95% of pods have turned yellow-brown. Dry to 12% moisture before storage. Soybeans have strong demand from oil processors and animal feed manufacturers.
response_tw: Soya y…õ ab…îdwe…õ a …õde sika ba pa ara. To akyi a osu asi yie. De Bradyrhizobium bacteria ka aba ho ansa na woto (eyi fi nitrogen na …õtumi ma nnuaba da sor mpr…õ mmi…õnsa). Gyae asukyer…õ s…õntimita 30-45 w…î ntam ne s…õntimita 60 w…î nnua so ntam. De phosphorus ferefere to mu bere a wode to. Tia nhaban foforo bere a …õto so mmi…õnsa no nnawotwe baako. Gye bere a 95% fidie no adan hoho b…î…î. Yaw kosi 12% nsuku ansa na waguina. Soya hia y…õ k…õse w…î ngo adwuma kuo ne mmoa aduan adwuma kuo ho.

instruction_en: How do I grow sweet potato successfully?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mekura asikyire bankye yie?
response_en: Sweet potato grows best in well-drained sandy loam soil. Plant vine cuttings (30-40 cm long) not seeds, inserting 2/3 of the cutting into the soil. Space 30 cm apart on ridges 90 cm apart. Apply NPK at planting. Weed early. Harvest 3-5 months after planting when leaves begin to yellow. Sweet potato is drought-tolerant and can grow in poor soils ‚Äî excellent for climate resilience. The leaves are also nutritious and can be eaten as vegetables.
response_tw: Asikyire bankye da sor pa w…î asase a nsuo pue mu yie na …õy…õ asinti…õ loam. To vine cuttings (s…õntimita 30-40 tenten) na mfa aba, de 2/3 w…î cutting no to asase mu. Gyae asukyer…õ s…õntimita 30 w…î ntwaho so a …õgyae s…õntimita 90. De NPK to mu bere a wode to. Tia nhaban foforo nt…õm. Gye abosome 3-5 akyi a wode to mu bere a nhaban reredan hoho. Asikyire bankye tumi nsuo pa ase hy…õ na …õtumi da sor w…î asase a enni pa ‚Äî …õy…õ …îkor…î pa ara ma katawere mu s…õsae ho. Nhaban no nso w…î aduan ho nhwiren ne wobetumi di s…õ nnuan.

instruction_en: How do I grow pineapple commercially?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mekura abor…îfo w…î adwuma mu?
response_en: Commercial pineapple production: plant suckers or crowns from healthy plants, space 60x30 cm on raised beds in rows, apply NPK fertilizer 3 times per year, force flowering by applying ethephon or acetylene solution at 12-14 months to synchronize harvest, control mealy bugs and heart rot disease, harvest 15-18 months after planting. MD2 and Smooth Cayenne are popular export varieties. Ghana exports significant volumes of pineapple to Europe.
response_tw: Abor…îfo adwuma kura ho: to suckers anaa crowns fi nnua a w…îw…î aho w…î, gyae asukyer…õ s…õntimita 60x30 w…î raised beds w…î nnua so mu, de NPK ferefere to mpr…õ mmi…õnsa bere biara, ma abode ba w…î de ethephon anaa acetylene solution hy…õ w…î bosome 12-14 akyi s…õ wogye bere koro, kora mealy bug ne heart rot yade…õ ase, gye bosome 15-18 akyi a wode to mu. MD2 ne Smooth Cayenne y…õ export aba a w…îp…õ w…în. Ghana de abor…îfo k…î Yurop dodo.

instruction_en: How do I grow moringa and what are its benefits?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mekura moringa na mfaaso b…õn na …õw…î?
response_en: Moringa is one of the most nutritious plants in the world and grows easily in Ghana. Plant seeds directly or use stem cuttings in well-drained soil. It tolerates drought and poor soils. Harvest leaves regularly to keep the tree short and productive. Moringa leaves can be eaten fresh, dried, and powdered. Benefits include: extremely high in iron, vitamins A and C, protein, and calcium. It is used medicinally and sold as a nutritional supplement at premium prices.
response_tw: Moringa y…õ nnua a …õw…î aduan ho nhwiren pa ara w…î wiase mu na …õda sor yie w…î Ghana. To aba t…ît…î anaa fa tibo cutting w…î asase a nsuo pue mu yie. ∆êtumi nsuo pa ne asase a enni pa ase hy…õ. Gye nhaban p…õ s…õ …õkora dua no tia ne adwuma mu. Moringa nhaban wobetumi di foforo, yaw na tutu. Mfaaso ho ne: iron, vitamins A ne C, protein, ne calcium ho y…õ dodo pa ara. W…îde di dawuro adwuma na t…în s…õ nutritional supplement w…î bo…î k…õse.

instruction_en: How do I manage disease in my cocoa nursery?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na metumi kora yade…õ ase w…î m'kookoo nursery mu?
response_en: Common cocoa nursery diseases include: collar rot (Phytophthora), damping off, and black pod. Control by: using sterilized potting mix, ensuring good drainage in nursery bags, avoiding overwatering, removing and destroying diseased seedlings immediately, maintaining good spacing for air circulation, spraying with copper-based fungicides preventatively, and ensuring nursery shade cloth allows 50% light penetration.
response_tw: Kookoo nursery yade…õ a w…îtaa hu ne: collar rot (Phytophthora), damping off, ne black pod. Kora ase w…î: fa potting mix a w…îasi amu, kora nsuo pue mu yie w…î nursery bags, b…î hw…õ nsuo dodo, yi ne seaa nnuaba mfoforo a w…îayar…õ nt…õm, kora asukyer…õ yie s…õ mframa pue mu, guare w…î copper-based fungicide s…õ kora ase ansa na yade…õ reba, na kora s…õ nursery shade cloth ma owia 50% pue mu.

instruction_en: What are the best practices for growing garden eggs?
instruction_tw: Anamm…în b…õn na …õy…õ pa w…î kura garden egg (Afrika ntr…îba)?
response_en: Garden eggs grow best in warm conditions with well-drained fertile soil. Start seeds in a nursery and transplant 4-6 week old seedlings. Space 60x60 cm. Apply NPK at planting and top-dress with urea at 4 weeks. Stake plants when they get large. Watch for mites, aphids, and flea beetles. Harvest when fruits are still firm and glossy ‚Äî over-ripe fruits become bitter. Garden eggs are popular in Ghanaian cuisine and have strong local market demand.
response_tw: Garden egg da sor pa w…î ahuhuro mu na asase a nsuo pue mu yie ne aduan pa. Fi aba fi nursery na si nnuaba mfoforo a w…îdin nnawotwe 4-6 ananmu. Gyae asukyer…õ s…õntimita 60x60. De NPK to mu bere a wode to na de urea to so nnawotwe 4 akyi. Si aduro nnua no bere a w…îrey…õ k…õse. Hw…õ mite, aphid, ne flea beetle ho. Gye bere a aba no da den na hyer…õn ‚Äî aba a …õawi dodo y…õ d…õm den. Garden egg p…õ pa ara w…î Ghana …îman mu aduan ho na …õw…î aguabasa hia k…õse.

instruction_en: How do I write a simple farm business plan?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mey…õ afuo adwuma dwumadie plan ketewa?
response_en: A simple farm business plan includes what you will grow and how much, land available, inputs needed and their costs, expected yield and selling price, total expected income, total costs, and expected profit. Also include risks and how you will manage them.
response_tw: Afuo adwuma dwumadie plan ketewa mu w…î: dea wob…õkura ne s…õn, asase a wow…î, adenne…õ a hia ne w…în bo…î, nnuaba a wob…õnya ne t…în bo…î, sika nyinaa a wob…õnya, sika nyinaa a wode, ne mfaaso a wob…õnya. Ka ns…îhw…õ ne …õde…õn na wob…õy…õ s…õ wob…õhw…õ won ase.

instruction_en: How do I find buyers for my farm produce before I harvest?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mehwehw…õ ade…õt…îfo ma m'afuo nnuaba ansa na megye w…în?
response_en: Visit local markets to identify traders. Contact food processing companies and restaurants. Use phone or WhatsApp groups for farmers in your area. Signing contract farming agreements with companies before the season guarantees buyers and sometimes provides inputs on credit.
response_tw: K…î aguabasa a …õb…õn wo s…õ wuhu nnipa a w…ît…în. Ka kyer…õ aduan y…õ ade…õ kuo ne adidibea. Fa f…în anaa WhatsApp kuo ma okuafo w…î wo beae. De contract farming agreement ka ade…õt…î kuo bere a bere mfiase ansa na …õma ade…õt…îfo y…õ dada.

instruction_en: What is contract farming and how does it benefit me?
instruction_tw: D…õn na contract farming y…õ na …õboa me s…õn?
response_en: Contract farming is an agreement between a farmer and a buyer before the season starts. The buyer agrees to purchase your produce at a fixed price after harvest. Benefits include guaranteed market, sometimes free inputs on credit, and technical support.
response_tw: Contract farming y…õ apam a …õda okuafo ne ade…õt…î kuo ntam ansa na bere no aba. Ade…õt…î kuo no ka ho s…õ w…îb…õt…î wo nnuaba bo…î a w…îaka akyi a wogye. Mfaaso ho ne: aguabasa a w…îadaa ho, adenne…õ a w…îde br…õ wo ka mu mmere bi, ne nnwuma boa.

instruction_en: What training is available for someone starting farming for the first time?
instruction_tw: Adesua b…õn na wob…õma aberantemmaa a …îresi okuafo adwuma …õkan?
response_en: Start small and learn as you grow. Choose one or two crops you understand well for your first season. Keep records of everything. Seek advice from experienced farmers and extension officers. Join a farmer group or cooperative. Do not give up after one bad season. Farming is a dignified and profitable business when done well.
response_tw: Fi ketewa na sua bere a woda sor. Paw nnuaba baako anaa abien a wohu w…în yie bere a …õdi …õkan. Kora ns…õm nyinaa. Hwehw…õ nnwuma boa fi okuafo a w…înim adwuma no ne extension officers. Ba okuafo kuo mu. Mma bere koro a aannya yie no amma wo anhuri. Okuafo adwuma y…õ adwuma a …õw…î anuonyam na …õde sika ba s…õ woy…õ no yie.

instruction_en: Should I register my farm as a business?
instruction_tw: ∆ês…õ s…õ mede m'afuo nkyer…õw?
response_en: Registering your farm with MOFA gives you access to government subsidies, training, extension services, credit, and market linkages. Visit your local MOFA office or District Assembly with your ID and land documents. Registration is free and very beneficial.
response_tw: Wo afuo nkyer…õw w…î MOFA de tumi ma wo s…õ wunya …îman ho boa, nyansap…î, extension nnwuma, ka, ne aguabasa ho ky…õfa. K…î wo MOFA ofisi anaa District Assembly a …õb…õn wo w…î wo nkyer…õws…õm ne asase krataa. Nkyer…õw y…õ daa na …õboa pa ara.

instruction_en: How do I get certified to sell organic produce?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na menya kratafa s…õ met…în organic nnuaba?
response_en: To sell as certified organic you need to farm organically for at least 3 years and pass inspection by an accredited certification body. In Ghana organizations like ENCOT and Control Union offer organic certification.
response_tw: S…õ wohwehw…õ t…în organic nnuaba a w…îakyer…õw w…în a, …õs…õ s…õ woy…õ organic okuafo adwuma mfe 3 p…õ na wufa inspection a organic kuo y…õ. W…î Ghana, kuo te s…õ ENCOT ne Control Union de organic kratafa br…õ.

instruction_en: What is GlobalGAP certification and how does it help farmers export?
instruction_tw: D…õn na GlobalGAP kratafa y…õ na …õboa okuafo s…õ w…îde nnuaba k…î amanaman?
response_en: GlobalGAP (Good Agricultural Practices) is an international certification standard that verifies produce is grown safely and sustainably. It is required by most European supermarkets to import fresh produce. Getting certified opens doors to premium export markets. Requirements include: safe pesticide use with records, worker welfare, environmental protection, and food safety. In Ghana, groups like PFAG (Pesticide and Fertilizer Association of Ghana) can help farmers get certified.
response_tw: GlobalGAP (Good Agricultural Practices) y…õ amanaman kratafa nhy…õso a …õkyer…õ s…õ nnuaba kura yie na …õho tumi. Yurop supermarkets bebree hia s…õ na w…îde nnuaba foforo ba. Nya kratafa no bue aguabasa k…õse a w…ît…în w…în bo…î k…õse. Nhy…õso ho ne: adwummakers dawuro fa a …õho tumi ne ns…õm kora, adwumafo ho hw…õ, …õwim ho hw…õ, ne aduan ho tumi. W…î Ghana, kuo te s…õ PFAG (Pesticide and Fertilizer Association of Ghana) tumi boa okuafo nya kratafa.

instruction_en: How do I register my farm as a business in Ghana?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mede m'afuo kyer…õw s…õ adwuma w…î Ghana?
response_en: To register your farm as a business in Ghana: register with the Registrar General's Department as a sole proprietorship or partnership (costs GHS 150-300), get a Tax Identification Number (TIN) from the Ghana Revenue Authority, register with MOFA for agricultural activities, open a business bank account, and get all required operating permits from your local assembly. A formally registered farm business is more credible to banks, buyers, and government programs.
response_tw: S…õ wohwehw…õ kyer…õw wo afuo s…õ adwuma w…î Ghana a: kyer…õw w…î Registrar General's Department s…õ sole proprietorship anaa partnership (sika GHS 150-300), nya Tax Identification Number (TIN) fi Ghana Revenue Authority h…î, kyer…õw w…î MOFA ho ma okuafo adwuma, bue adwuma ban account, na nya kratafa biara a wohia fi wo local assembly. Afuo adwuma a w…îakyer…õw no y…õ pa ara w…î ban, ade…õt…îfo, ne …îman adwuma ho.

instruction_en: What is Fairtrade certification and what are its benefits for farmers?
instruction_tw: D…õn na Fairtrade kratafa y…õ na mfaaso b…õn na …õw…î ho ma okuafo?
response_en: Fairtrade certification guarantees farmers a minimum price for their produce that covers production costs plus a social premium that is invested in community development. Benefits include: stable income even when world prices drop, Fairtrade premium funds for schools, health, and infrastructure, access to pre-financing before harvest, better trading relationships, and marketing support. Fairtrade is common in Ghana for cocoa, coffee, and fresh fruit. Contact Fairtrade Africa for information.
response_tw: Fairtrade kratafa h…î kura s…õ okuafo nya bo…î a …õdata w…în nnuaba a …õkata adwuma ho sika ne social premium a w…îde fa oman nkosuo mu. Mfaaso ho ne: sika a …õda p…õ bere a amanaman bo…î tow, Fairtrade premium sika ma sukuu, yade…õ, ne nkosuo adwuma, nya sika ansa na wogye nnuaba, adwuma ho apam a …õy…õ pa, ne adwuma ho boa. Fairtrade p…õ w…î Ghana ho ma kookoo, k…îfi, ne aba foforo. Ka kyer…õ Fairtrade Africa s…õ wunya ns…õm.

instruction_en: How do I get a licence to sell agricultural chemicals in Ghana?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na menya kratafa s…õ met…în okuafo adwuma chemicals w…î Ghana?
response_en: To sell agricultural chemicals (pesticides, fertilizers) in Ghana, you need: a license from the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), which requires completing a training course on pesticide handling, passing an examination, paying the licensing fee, having appropriate storage facilities, and renewing the license annually. Selling agricultural chemicals without a license is illegal and carries penalties. Contact the EPA Pesticides Control and Management Centre for the application process.
response_tw: S…õ wohwehw…õ t…în okuafo adwuma chemicals (adwumakers dawuro, ferefere) w…î Ghana a, wohia: kratafa fi Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), a …õhia s…õ wo siesie adwummakers dawuro hw…õ suahu, fa exam mu, tua kratafa sika, w…î guina fie a …õfata, na sane kratafa mfe biara. T…în okuafo adwuma chemicals a wonni kratafa y…õ illegal na …õw…î akyinnye. Ka kyer…õ EPA Pesticides Control and Management Centre s…õ wuhu bisa anamm…în.

instruction_en: What information do I need to qualify for an agricultural loan?
instruction_tw: Ns…õm b…õn na …õs…õ mekora s…õ menya okuafo adwuma ka?
response_en: To qualify for agricultural loans, banks and microfinance institutions typically require: farm records showing area farmed and crops grown, sales records for at least one season, input purchase receipts, a farm business plan, MOFA registration certificate, land ownership or lease documents, a valid ID, and sometimes a guarantor. Start keeping records now even if you are not currently seeking a loan ‚Äî it takes time to build a credible financial history that banks trust.
response_tw: S…õ wohwehw…õ nya okuafo adwuma ka a, ban ne microfinance institutions taa hia: afuo ns…õm a …õkyer…õ asase asukyer…õ ne nnuaba a wokura, t…în ns…õm ma bere baako p…õ p…õ, adenne…õ t…în krataa, afuo adwuma dwumadie plan, MOFA kyer…õws…õm kratafa, asase wura anaa as…õ krataa, ID a …õtia, na mmere bi obi a …îb…õka ho. Fi kora ns…õm saa ara bere a wonhwehw…õ ka da ‚Äî …õhia bere s…õ si sika ho ns…õm a w…îgye di a ban tumi gye di.

instruction_en: How do I build a small dam or water harvesting system?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mesi dam ketewa anaa nsuo gye sist…õm?
response_en: Simple water harvesting includes digging half-moon catchments to collect rainwater, constructing check dams as small barriers across drainage channels, and building farm ponds lined with clay or plastic. Contact your local Water Resources Commission for technical support.
response_tw: Nsuo gye anamm…în ketewa ho ne: hu osu nsuo gye mm…înkyemmonkyem, si check dams te s…õ akwantempon ketewa w…î nsuo ntwaho nky…õn, na tu afuo pond a w…îde amane anaa plastik si faako mu. Ka kyer…õ wo Water Resources Commission s…õ wunya nnwuma boa.

instruction_en: My well has dried up in the dry season. How do I conserve water?
instruction_tw: M'esre yera w…î …îp…õ bere mu. ∆êde…õn na metumi guina nsuo dodo?
response_en: Construct a farm pond or dig a larger deeper well. Install a rainwater harvesting system collecting from rooftops into tanks during rainy season. Use water-efficient irrigation methods. Alternatively identify permanent water sources like rivers and use pumps.
response_tw: Tu afuo pond anaa hu esre k…õse ne tenten. Si osu nsuo gye sist…õm a w…îde fi ato so gye w…î tank mu w…î osu bere mu. Fa nsuo ho anamm…în a …õfa nsuo nky…õn. Anoa, hwehw…õ nsuo a …õda h…î p…õ te s…õ …îsub…înten na de pump fa di dwuma.

instruction_en: How do I conserve water on my farm during the dry season?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mekora nsuo w…î m'afuo mu w…î …îp…õ bere mu?
response_en: Conserve water by mulching heavily around plants to reduce evaporation, watering early morning or evening only, using drip irrigation which reduces water use by up to 60%, growing drought-tolerant crops, making windbreaks to reduce drying winds, and collecting and storing rainwater during wet season.
response_tw: Kora nsuo w…î de mulch to nnuaba ho papaapa s…õ nsukyer…õ ba sua, de nsuo to owia da ano anaa anwummere p…õ, fa drip irrigation a …õsua nsuo k…î 60%, kura nnuaba a …õtumi nsuo pa ase, si windbreaks s…õ mframa a …õma asase yera nk…î, na gye ne guina osu nsuo w…î osu bere mu.

instruction_en: What equipment do I need to start a small farm?
instruction_tw: Ade…õ b…õn na mehia s…õ mesi afuo ketewa?
response_en: Essential tools for smallholder farming include a hoe and cutlass for land preparation and weeding, a watering can for irrigation, a knapsack sprayer for pesticide application, a measuring tape, storage bags including hermetic bags for grain, and a simple scale.
response_tw: Ade…õ a …õhia w…î afuo ketewa mu ne ak…îde ne sekan ma asase siesie ne nhaban tia, nsuo ky…õ ma nsuo to, knapsack sprayer ma dawuro guare, susudua, guina bags a hermetic bags ka ho ma nnuaba, ne scale ketewa ma nnuaba sus.

instruction_en: How do I maintain my knapsack sprayer?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mehw…õ m'knapsack sprayer so?
response_en: After each use, rinse the tank three times with clean water and spray the wash water away from crops. Check and replace worn nozzles regularly. Lubricate the pump piston periodically. Store empty and clean in a cool dry area. Regularly inspect hoses and connections for leaks.
response_tw: Wodi dwuma biara akyi, hohoro nkuku no mpr…õ mmi…õnsa w…î nsuo horo na guare nsuo a wode hohoro no ho afi nnuaba ho. Hw…õ na sesa nozzle a as…õe p…õ. De ngo hy…õ pump no mu bere bi. Guina a …õhoro w…î bae a …õhuru na yera. Hw…õ hose ne nn…î…îmu p…õ.

instruction_en: What is an agricultural extension officer and how can they help me?
instruction_tw: D…õn na agricultural extension officer y…õ?
response_en: Agricultural extension officers from MOFA provide free advice and training to farmers. They can visit your farm to diagnose crop problems, recommend solutions, inform you about new varieties and technologies, connect you to government programs, and help you access subsidized inputs.
response_tw: Agricultural extension officers fi MOFA de boa ne nyansap…î br…õ okuafo aky…õ. W…îbetumi ba wo afuo so s…õ wuhw…õ nnuaba ns…õm, ka boa kyer…õ, kaa wo tia aba ne technology fofor…î ho, kyer…õ wo …îman adwuma, na boa wo nya adenne…õ a w…îatwa w…în bo…î.

instruction_en: How do I access government agricultural support in Ghana?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na menya …îman okuafo adwuma boa?
response_en: Register with your local MOFA office and stay in contact with your extension officer. Subsidies like subsidized fertilizer and seeds under PFJ are distributed through MOFA-registered channels. Have your Ghana Card or voter ID ready and register early as subsidies are often limited.
response_tw: Kyer…õw wo din w…î wo MOFA ofisi a …õb…õn wo na tena wo extension officer ho. Boa te s…õ ferefere ne aba a w…îatwa bo…î w…î PFJ ase no pue w…î MOFA mu. Wura wo Ghana Card anaa …îman nkyer…õws…õm na kyer…õw wo din nt…õm efis…õ boa no taa y…õ ketewa.

instruction_en: What is the difference between hybrid seeds and open-pollinated seeds?
instruction_tw: Hybrid aba ne open-pollinated aba ntam y…õ d…õn?
response_en: Hybrid seeds are bred from two parent lines to produce vigorous high-yielding crops. However seeds saved from hybrids do not perform well in the next season ‚Äî you must buy fresh seeds each time. Open-pollinated varieties allow you to save seeds for replanting.
response_tw: W…îde aba abien ntam b…î hybrid aba s…õ …õma nnuaba a w…îw…î aho…îden na w…îda sor dodo. Nanso aba a wogye fi hybrid no nnua mu nti adwuma yie bere a …õto so abien ‚Äî …õs…õ s…õ wot…î aba foforo bere biara. Open-pollinated aba de tumi gye aba s…õ wode to mu bio.

instruction_en: How do I save my own seeds for replanting next season?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na megye m'aba s…õ mede to mu w…î bere a …õto so?
response_en: For open-pollinated varieties select the best-performing plants and allow their fruits to fully mature on the plant. Harvest seeds, clean and dry them thoroughly in shade. Store in airtight containers with silica gel sachets to absorb moisture. Label with crop name, variety, and date.
response_tw: Open-pollinated aba ho, paw nnua a w…îda sor pa na ma w…în aba no wie ase yie w…î nnua so. Gye aba no, hohoro na yaw w…î owia sum mu yie. Guina w…î nkuku a apo so ne silica gel sachets a …õsuo nsukuro. Kyer…õw nnuaba din, aba din ne da so.

instruction_en: How do I practise farming that helps reduce climate change?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mey…õ okuafo adwuma a …õboa katawere mu s…õsae so?
response_en: Practice climate-smart farming by planting trees on your farm through agroforestry, using compost and manure instead of chemical fertilizers, practicing minimum tillage to protect soil carbon, using water-efficient irrigation, growing diverse crops, and avoiding burning crop residues.
response_tw: Y…õ climate-smart farming w…î to nnua w…î wo afuo so w…î agroforestry ho, de compost ne aguare fa ahemfie ferefere ananmu, y…õ minimum tillage s…õ …õkora asase carbon, fa nsuo-efficient irrigation, kura nnuaba ahorow, na b…î hw…õ hyew nnuaba nsennua.

instruction_en: Which crops are most resilient to climate change in Ghana?
instruction_tw: Nnuaba b…õn na …õtumi wiase tebea s…õsae ase hy…õ yie w…î Ghana?
response_en: Drought-tolerant crops for Ghana's changing climate include improved NERICA rice, drought-tolerant maize varieties like Obatanpa and Omankwa, cowpea, sorghum, millet, and cassava. Contact CSIR-SARI or MOFA for the latest recommended varieties in your region.
response_tw: Nnuaba a …õtumi nsuo pa ase hy…õ w…î Ghana tebea s…õsae mu ne NERICA …îm…î a w…îay…õ no foforo, aburow a …õtumi nsuo pa ase te s…õ Obatanpa ne Omankwa, ab…îdwe…õ, …ît…õ, …ît…õ pa, ne bankye. Ka kyer…õ CSIR-SARI anaa MOFA s…õ wunya aba a w…îaka.

instruction_en: What is agroforestry and how does it benefit my farm?
instruction_tw: D…õn na agroforestry y…õ na …õboa m'afuo s…õn?
response_en: Agroforestry is growing trees alongside crops or livestock on the same land. Benefits include: trees provide shade that reduces water stress, tree roots hold soil and prevent erosion, fallen leaves add organic matter, some trees fix nitrogen improving soil fertility, trees provide fruit or timber as additional income, and trees help store carbon reducing climate change impact. Recommended trees include Moringa, Leucaena, and Acacia.
response_tw: Agroforestry y…õ kura nnua w…î nnuaba anaa mmoa ho w…î asase koro so. Mfaaso ho ne: nnua de ewim br…õ ma …õma nsuo hia nk…îm, nnua ntini kora asase na …õmma asase nhuru, nhaban a atu de nhwiren foforo ka ho, nnua bi fi nitrogen ma asase ho nhwiren da sor, nnua de aba anaa dua bi ka ho s…õ sika fofor…î, na nnua boa guina carbon na …õsua katawere mu s…õsae ho …îk…îm. Nnua a w…îka w…în ho ne Moringa, Leucaena, ne Acacia.

instruction_en: How do I prepare my farm for unpredictable weather?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mesiesie m'afuo ama …õwim tebea a …õntumi nnim?
response_en: To prepare for unpredictable weather: grow more than one crop to spread risk, keep records of past planting dates and yields to understand your local patterns, store enough food and seed reserves, install or improve water harvesting systems, use drought and flood-tolerant varieties, maintain healthy soil with high organic matter which buffers both drought and flooding, and join farmer groups that share weather information.
response_tw: Siesie ama …õwim tebea a …õntumi nnim: kura nnuaba dodo s…õ mfomso antete, kora aba to bere ne nnuaba a wogye ho ns…õm s…õ wuhu wo beae tebea, guina aduan ne aba ahw…õso, si anaa siesie nsuo guina syst…õm, fa aba a …õtumi nsuo pa ne nsuo tenten ase hy…õ, kora asase a enni aho yie ne nhwiren foforo dodo a …õboa w…î nsuo pa ne nsuo tenten bere, na ba okuafo kuo a w…îky…õ …õwim amanne…õ.

instruction_en: What is minimum tillage and how is it good for the soil?
instruction_tw: D…õn na minimum tillage y…õ na …õde…õn na …õy…õ asase pa?
response_en: Minimum tillage (also called conservation tillage) means disturbing the soil as little as possible when preparing for planting ‚Äî using just enough tillage to create a seedbed. Benefits include: preserves soil structure, reduces erosion, maintains soil moisture, protects soil organisms, reduces fuel and labor costs, and helps store carbon in the soil. Use a jab planter or hand hoe to plant directly into the soil with minimal disturbance.
response_tw: Minimum tillage (a w…îfre no conservation tillage nso) kyer…õ haw asase ketewa ketewa bere a wosiesie aba to mu ansa ‚Äî fa tillage a …õdodo p…õ s…õ woyi asase aba to mu ansa. Mfaaso ho ne: kora asase mu siesie, sua erosion, kora nsukuro w…î asase mu, kora asase mu nkwa, sua fuel ne dwumadie ho sika, na boa guina carbon w…î asase mu. Fa jab planter anaa nsa ak…îde to aba t…ît…î w…î asase mu na wonhaw dodo.

instruction_en: How does planting trees on my farm help reduce climate change?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na to nnua w…î m'afuo so boa katawere mu s…õsae so?
response_en: Trees absorb carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and store it in their wood and roots ‚Äî this is called carbon sequestration. A single large tree can absorb 22 kg of carbon dioxide per year. Trees also reduce local temperatures, increase rainfall through transpiration, protect water catchments, and reduce erosion. Planting trees on your farm is one of the most powerful climate actions a farmer can take.
response_tw: Nnua gye carbon dioxide fi …õwim mu na guina mu w…î w…în dua ne ntini mu ‚Äî w…îfre eyi carbon sequestration. Dua k…õse baako tumi gye carbon dioxide kilogram 22 w…î afe biara. Nnua nso sua beae ahuhuro, boa osu ba w…î transpiration mu, kora nsuo gye beae, na sua erosion. To nnua w…î wo afuo so y…õ katawere mu s…õsae dwuma titiriw a okuafo tumi y…õ.

instruction_en: What is solar-powered irrigation and can a small farmer use it?
instruction_tw: D…õn na solar powered irrigation y…õ na okuafo ketewa tumi di?
response_en: Solar powered irrigation uses solar panels to generate electricity that powers water pumps for irrigating crops. Benefits include: no fuel costs, low maintenance, reliable operation, and environmental friendliness. Small farmers can access it through: government subsidy programs, cooperative ownership of shared solar pumps, microfinance loans for solar pump purchase, and NGO programs. The initial cost is high but long-term savings are significant.
response_tw: Solar powered irrigation fa solar panels di dwuma s…õ …õy…õ electricity a …õma nsuo pumps a w…îde to nnuaba so adwuma. Mfaaso ho ne: fuel ho sika nni, hw…õ ase y…õ mmer…õ, adwuma y…õ p…õ, na …õma …õwim no da yie. Okuafo ketewa tumi nya ase w…î: …îman boa adwuma, cooperative bom fa solar pumps, microfinance ka ma solar pump t…în, ne NGO adwuma. Sika a wode fi no y…õ den nanso bere tenten ho mfaaso y…õ k…õse.

instruction_en: How do I use my livestock to benefit my crop farming?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mefa m'mmoa di dwuma ma m'afuo nnuaba?
response_en: Integrate livestock by using their manure as organic fertilizer for crops. Chickens can help control insects and weeds. After harvest allow animals to graze on crop residues which also helps clear the field. This integrated farming approach reduces costs and improves productivity.
response_tw: Ka mmoa ho w…î de w…în aguare y…õ nhwiren foforo ferefere ma nnuaba. Akr…îma tumi boa kora adwummakers ne nhaban foforo ase. Nnuaba gye akyi, ma mmoa nom nnuaba nsennua a aka no a …õnso boa hohoro afuo no. Eyi farming anamm…în sua sika na …õma afuo no nyinaa da sor yie.

instruction_en: My chickens are dying suddenly. What could be causing this?
instruction_tw: M'akr…îma rewu nt…õm. D…õn na betumi y…õ?
response_en: Sudden chicken deaths could be from Newcastle Disease, Gumboro disease, or poisoning. Vaccinate chickens against common diseases at the right ages. Consult a veterinary officer immediately for diagnosis. Quarantine sick birds to prevent spread to healthy ones.
response_tw: Akr…îma wu a …õba nt…õm betumi fi Newcastle Yade…õ, Gumboro yade…õ, anaa dawuro. De akr…îma k…î yade…õ dawuro bere a …õfata w…în mfe…õ so. Ka veterinary officer ho nt…õm s…õ …îhw…õ yade…õ no. Yi akr…îma a w…îayar…õ no fi w…în a w…îw…î aho no mu.

instruction_en: How do I prevent disease from spreading among my animals?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mema yade…õ antete w…î m'mmoa ntam?
response_en: Vaccinate all animals on schedule, quarantine new animals for 2 weeks before mixing with your herd, isolate sick animals immediately, maintain clean housing with good ventilation, dispose of dead animals properly, and consult a veterinary officer regularly. Good hygiene is the most important disease prevention tool.
response_tw: De mmoa nyinaa k…î yade…õ dawuro bere a …õs…õ, yi mmoa foforo na w…întena nnawotwe 2 ansa na woka w…în ne mmoa a w…îw…î h…î bom, yi mmoa a w…îayar…õ nt…õm ara, kora w…în fie a …õhoro ne mframa pue mu yie, yi mmoa a awu yie, na ka veterinary officer ho p…õ.

instruction_en: What farming apps are useful for farmers in Ghana?
instruction_tw: Farming apps b…õn na …õboa okuafo w…î Ghana?
response_en: Useful apps for Ghanaian farmers include: Esoko for market prices, Farmerline for agricultural advice and services, Hello Tractor for renting tractors, AgroFresh for produce quality management, Google Translate for translating agricultural content, YouTube for farming tutorials, Ghana Met Agency app for weather forecasts, and WhatsApp for connecting with other farmers and extension officers. Most are free or low-cost.
response_tw: Apps a …õboa okuafo Ghana mu ne: Esoko ma aguabasa bo…î, Farmerline ma okuafo adwuma boa ne nnwuma, Hello Tractor ma tractor as…õ, AgroFresh ma nnuaba ho pa hw…õ, Google Translate ma okuafo ns…õm dan, YouTube ma okuafo adwuma nhyehy…õe, Ghana Met Agency app ma …õwim amanne…õ, ne WhatsApp ma kasa ne okuafo ne extension officers fofor…î. W…în bebree y…õ daa anaa bo…î y…õ ketewa.

instruction_en: How do I use YouTube to learn modern farming techniques?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mefa YouTube di dwuma s…õ sua okuafo adwuma anamm…în?
response_en: YouTube has thousands of free farming tutorials. Search for: specific crop names plus 'farming Ghana', pest or disease names plus 'how to control', fertilizer application techniques, irrigation setup guides, and post-harvest handling. Subscribe to channels like IITA, CTA Agriculture, and local Ghanaian farming channels. Download videos on WiFi to watch offline when you are at the farm without internet access.
response_tw: YouTube w…î okuafo adwuma nhyehy…õe ahorow a …õy…õ daa. Hwehw…õ: nnuaba din ne 'farming Ghana', adwumakers anaa yade…õ din ne 'how to control', ferefere to anamm…în, nsuo to nhyehy…õe, ne gye akyi hw…õ ase anamm…în. Kyer…õw wo din w…î channels te s…õ IITA, CTA Agriculture, ne Ghana okuafo channels. Sii videos w…î WiFi so s…õ wuhw…õ w…în bere a wow…î afuo so na internet nni h…î.

instruction_en: What is a soil testing kit and how do I use it?
instruction_tw: D…õn na soil testing kit y…õ na …õde…õn na mefa di dwuma?
response_en: A soil testing kit is a set of chemicals and test strips or color charts that let you test your soil's pH, nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium levels at home. To use: collect soil from several spots in your field and mix together, take a small sample, follow the kit instructions to mix with testing chemicals, compare the color change to the chart provided, and use results to decide what fertilizer to apply. Kits are available at agricultural supply stores.
response_tw: Soil testing kit y…õ chemicals ne test strips anaa color charts a …õma wo tumi hw…õ wo asase pH, nitrogen, phosphorus, ne potassium w…î fie. Di dwuma eyi: gye asase fi beae ahodo…î w…î afuo mu na ka bom, fa sample ketewa, bu kit nhy…õso so s…õ de chemicals ka ho, twe rere s…õsae na ka ho ne chart a w…îde br…õ, na fa adwuma ho s…õ paw ferefere a wohia. Kits w…î h…î w…î agricultural supply ade…õt…î h…î.

instruction_en: What is the role of women in agriculture in Ghana?
instruction_tw: D…õn na mmaa gyina w…î okuafo adwuma mu w…î Ghana?
response_en: Women play a critical role in Ghanaian agriculture ‚Äî they make up about 52% of the agricultural workforce. Women are primarily responsible for food crop production, post-harvest processing, and marketing. They often face challenges accessing land, credit, and inputs. Programs that specifically support women farmers include Women in Agricultural Development (WIAD) under MOFA, and many NGOs focusing on women's empowerment in agriculture.
response_tw: Mmaa gyina titiriw pa ara w…î Ghana okuafo adwuma mu ‚Äî w…îy…õ b…õn 52% w…î okuafo adwumafo mu. Mmaa hia aduan nnuaba kura, gye akyi y…õ adwuma, ne t…în. W…îtaa hia asase, ka, ne adenne…õ ho ns…õm. Adwuma a …õhw…õ mmaa okuafo ho p…õ ne Women in Agricultural Development (WIAD) w…î MOFA ase, ne NGOs bebree a w…îhw…õ mmaa ho w…î okuafo adwuma mu.

instruction_en: How do I use my smartphone camera to identify problems on my crops?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mefa smartphone camera di dwuma s…õ hunu m'nnuaba ns…õm?
response_en: Take clear close-up photos of affected plant parts ‚Äî leaves, stems, fruits, or roots. Include a coin for scale. Send photos to: your extension officer via WhatsApp, post in farmer WhatsApp or Facebook groups for advice, use apps like Plantix or PlantVillage that use AI to diagnose diseases from photos, or email to your nearest CSIR research station. Clear photos from multiple angles help get the most accurate diagnosis.
response_tw: Fa foto fitafita w…î nnuaba a w…îayar…õ no ho ‚Äî nhaban, tibo, aba, anaa ntini. Ka sika de hw…õ bo…î. De foto k…î: wo extension officer ho w…î WhatsApp, de si w…î okuafo WhatsApp anaa Facebook kuo mu s…õ wunya boa, fa apps te s…õ Plantix anaa PlantVillage a w…îde AI di dwuma s…õ hunu yade…õ fi foto mu, anaa email k…î CSIR research station a …õb…õn wo. Foto fitafita fi af√£ ahodo…î boa s…õ wuhu yade…õ no pa ara.

instruction_en: How do I set up a small poultry farm at home?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mesi akr…îma afuo ketewa w…î fie?
response_en: To start a small home poultry farm: decide on type (layers for eggs or broilers for meat), build or buy a simple wooden or wire coop with shade and good ventilation, start with 10-20 birds, provide clean water and commercial feed, vaccinate birds at the recommended ages, keep the coop clean, and record feeds, deaths, and egg production. Start small and scale as you learn.
response_tw: S…õ wohwehw…õ si akr…îma afuo ketewa w…î fie: paw ahodo…î (layers ma kosua anaa broilers ma nam), si anaa t…î dua anaa wire fie a ewim br…õ ne mframa pue mu yie, fi akr…îma 10-20, de nsuo horo ne aduan a w…ît…î ma w…în, de akr…îma k…î yade…õ dawuro bere a …õs…õ, kora fie no horo, na kora aduan, wuu, ne kosua ho ns…õm. Fi ketewa na fa dodo so bere a wosua.

instruction_en: How do I get the most milk from my cows?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na metumi ma m'boo nufuo pa ara?
response_en: Improve cow milk quality and quantity by: feeding balanced rations including good pasture, silage, hay, and concentrate feed; providing clean water always; milking at the same time every day; maintaining good hygiene during milking; treating and preventing mastitis (udder inflammation) promptly; providing shade and reducing stress; and selecting productive dairy breeds or crossing local cattle with improved breeds.
response_tw: Siesie boo nufuo ho pa ne k…õse w…î: ma aduan a …õp…õ w…în ho yie a mu w…î at…õnt…õnni pa, silage, hay, ne concentrate aduan; de nsuo horo ma w…în daa; nom w…în nufuo bere koro da biara; kora ho tumi bere a wonuom nufuo; y…õ dawuro ma na kora mastitis (nufuo mu yade…õ) nt…õm; ma ewim br…õ w…în na mma w…în nhaw; na paw boo a w…în nufuo y…õ pa anaa ka beae boo ne boo fofor…î a …õda sor bom.

instruction_en: What locally available feed ingredients are good for my livestock to save money?
instruction_tw: Aduan b…õn a …õw…î fie na …õy…õ pa w…î m'mmoa ho s…õ mpampa sika?
response_en: Reduce feed costs using local ingredients: for poultry, use maize germ, rice bran, palm kernel cake, fishmeal, and dried moringa leaves as protein; for pigs, use cassava peels, plantain peels, vegetable waste, and kitchen leftovers (no meat or salt); for cattle, plant improved pasture grasses like Brachiaria and Stylosanthes, use crop residues like maize stover and rice straw, and supplement with salt blocks.
response_tw: Sua aduan sika w…î fa beae adenne…õ: akr…îma ho, fa aburow germ, …îm…î nkutupan, abe nkuto cake, nsuomnam dust, ne moringa nhaban a ayaw s…õ aduan protein; prako ho, fa bankye nkutupan, bor…îd…î nkutupan, nnuan asennua, ne fie aduan a aka (mfa nam anaa …õwa); boo ho, to Brachiaria ne Stylosanthes at…õnt…õnni a w…îay…õ w…în foforo, fa nnuaba nsennua te s…õ aburow nsennua ne …îm…î tow, na ka salt blocks ho.

instruction_en: How do I properly treat sick animals on my farm?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mehw…õ mmoa a w…îayar…õ ho yie?
response_en: When handling sick animals: wear gloves and wash hands thoroughly after contact, isolate sick animals from healthy ones immediately, do not move sick animals to other farms, contact a veterinary officer for diagnosis before administering treatment, follow withdrawal periods for medicines before slaughter or selling milk, keep records of all treatments given, and dispose of dead animals by deep burial away from water sources.
response_tw: Bere a wuhw…õ mmoa a w…îayar…õ ho: fa nsatea nhoma na hohoro wo nsa yie akyi a woka w…în, yi mmoa a w…îayar…õ fi w…în a w…îw…î aho no mu nt…õm, mfa mmoa a w…îayar…õ k…î afuo fofor…î, ka veterinary officer ho s…õ …îhunu yade…õ no ansa na wode dawuro to, bu dawuro withdrawal bere ansa na wokum anaa t…în nufuo, kora ns…õm a y…õ dawuro nyinaa ho, na de mmoa a awu sisie w…î asase mu tenten fi nsuo ho.

instruction_en: What are the signs that my goat is pregnant and what should I do?
instruction_tw: Ahohyenam b…õn na …õkyer…õ s…õ m'abirekyi w…î nyins…õn na …õde…õn na mey…õ?
response_en: Signs of goat pregnancy include: no return to heat after mating, enlarged abdomen especially from month 3, enlarged udder especially in the last month, behavioral changes like becoming calmer, and weight gain. Care during pregnancy: provide extra feed especially in the last 6 weeks, ensure access to clean water always, avoid rough handling, vaccinate against clostridial diseases, deworm at least 3 weeks before kidding, and prepare a clean kidding area.
response_tw: Abirekyi nyins…õn ahohyenam ho ne: onsan nba bio akyi a watwa, yafunu k…õse titiriw fi bosome 3, nufuo k…õse titiriw w…î bosome …îkyena, …îtenabea s…õsae te s…õ y…õ abotare, na bo…î sor. Hw…õ bere nyins…õn mu: ma aduan dodo titiriw w…î nnawotwe 6 a atwam ansa na omba, kora nsuo horo w…î h…î daa, mhaw no, de k…î clostridial yade…õ dawuro, hw…õ adwummakers ase wiemu 3 ansa na oba mba, na siesie beae horo s…õ oba mba.

instruction_en: What nutritional guidelines should a farmer follow for their livestock?
instruction_tw: Aduan ho tumi anamm…în b…õn na okuafo s…õ bu w…în so?
response_en: Key food safety practices for farmers include: washing hands before handling produce, using clean water for irrigation especially on leafy vegetables, applying pesticides according to label instructions and respecting pre-harvest intervals, not using human waste as fertilizer, keeping animals away from vegetable gardens, using clean equipment and containers, and training farm workers on hygiene practices.
response_tw: Aduan ho tumi anamm…în a …õhia ma okuafo ne: hohoro wo nsa ansa na wohw…õ nnuaba ho, fa nsuo horo s…õ to nnuaba so titiriw nhaban nnuan so, de adwummakers dawuro to w…î krataa nhy…õso so na bu pre-harvest bere so, mfa onipa aguare y…õ ferefere, mma mmoa nnuaba afuo mu, fa egyapade ne nkuku horo, na kyer…õ afuo adwumafo aduan ho tumi anamm…în.

instruction_en: How long should I wait after spraying pesticide before I can harvest my crops?
instruction_tw: Bere ahe na …õs…õ me tw…õn akyi a mede adwummakers dawuro guare ansa na megye nnuaba?
response_en: The waiting time between pesticide application and harvest is called the Pre-Harvest Interval (PHI) and varies by chemical. Always check the pesticide label for the specific PHI. Common examples: Emamectin benzoate (3-7 days), Cypermethrin (7-14 days), Mancozeb fungicide (7 days), Glyphosate herbicide (14 days). If the label is lost, wait at least 14 days as a safe default. Never harvest before the PHI has passed.
response_tw: Bere a …õda adwummakers dawuro to ne nnuaba gye ntam s…õ Pre-Harvest Interval (PHI) na …õsesa w…î chemical biara so. Hw…õ adwummakers dawuro krataa so bere a …õhia p…õ. Nhw…õso a w…îtaa hu: Emamectin benzoate (nnafoa 3-7), Cypermethrin (nnafoa 7-14), Mancozeb fungicide (nnafoa 7), Glyphosate herbicide (nnafoa 14). S…õ krataa no ahu a, tw…õn nnafoa 14 p…õ s…õ beae a …õho tumi. Mye nnuaba ansa na PHI no awi da.

instruction_en: How do I properly wash vegetables before eating or selling them?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mehohoro nnuan yie ansa na midi anaa t…în?
response_en: Wash vegetables safely by: using clean potable water, soaking for 1-2 minutes in a solution of 1 tablespoon of vinegar per litre of water or approved food-grade chlorine solution, then rinsing thoroughly with clean water. For leafy vegetables, separate leaves and wash each leaf individually. Dry before packaging. Never wash with river, pond, or canal water as these may contain pathogens.
response_tw: Hohoro nnuan yie w…î: fa nsuo horo a w…îtumi nom, da w…î vinegar tablespoon baako w…î nsuo litre biara mu anaa food-grade chlorine a w…îagyei mu s…õnt…î 1-2, afei hohoro yie w…î nsuo horo. Nhaban nnuan ho, twa nhaban mu na hohoro nhaban biara biara ho. Yaw ansa na wopaka. Mhohoro w…î …îsub…înten, asranom, anaa k…ît…î nsuo mu da efis…õ w…în mu tumi w…î yade…õ.

instruction_en: Is there any risk in using human or animal urine as fertilizer?
instruction_tw: D…õn na …îk…îm w…î ho w…î fa onipa anaa mmoa aguare foforo y…õ ferefere?
response_en: Using raw (untreated) waste carries serious risks: human and animal waste contains pathogens like E.coli, Salmonella, and parasites that can contaminate crops and cause disease in people who eat them. The WHO recommends composting waste for at least 6-12 months or treating with ash or lime before applying to crops. Only apply to soil, never to the edible parts of plants. Always wash hands and produce thoroughly.
response_tw: Fa aguare foforo (a wonsi amu) di dwuma w…î …îk…îm k…õse: onipa ne mmoa aguare w…î yade…õ mu te s…õ E.coli, Salmonella, ne adwumakers a …õtumi haw nnuaba na y…õ nnipa a w…îdi w…în yadee. WHO ka s…õ compost aguare wiemu 6-12 anaa siesie w…î nkamfo anaa lime ho ansa na wode to nnuaba so. De to asase p…õ, mde to nnuaba a w…îdi ho da. Hohoro wo nsa ne nnuaba yie daa.

instruction_en: How do I read and understand a pesticide label?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mede adwummakers dawuro …îk…îm hwe krataa k…î?
response_en: If pesticide poisoning occurs: call the Ghana Poisons Information Centre or the nearest hospital immediately, remove the victim from the exposure area, if ingested do not induce vomiting unless directed by medical professionals, if on skin remove contaminated clothing and wash with soap and water, if in eyes flush with clean water for 15-20 minutes, bring the pesticide container or label to the hospital. Prevention: always wear protective equipment when using pesticides.
response_tw: S…õ adwummakers dawuro …îk…îm ba a: fr…õ Ghana Poisons Information Centre anaa sikabea a …õb…õn wo nt…õm, yi obi a …îk…îm aba no fi beae no, s…õ …înom a mma onyi no mua ansa na onyini …îk…îm ne mu, s…õ firi wo ho skin so a yi ntama a dawuro ato so na hohoro w…î sab…î ne nsuo mu, s…õ w…î ani mu a fa nsuo horo mu s…õnt…î 15-20, de adwummakers dawuro nkuku anaa krataa k…î sikabea. Kora ase: taa hye ntoma a …õkora wo ho bere a wode adwummakers dawuro di dwuma.

instruction_en: Where can I buy certified seeds in Ghana?
instruction_tw: ∆êhe na metumi t…î aba a w…îakyer…õw w…în w…î Ghana?
response_en: Buy certified seeds from: MOFA-approved agro-input dealers, agricultural supply stores in district capitals, certified seed companies like SeedCo, Pioneer, and Yara, cooperative input stores, and CSIR research stations which sometimes sell breeder or foundation seeds. Always look for the official seed certification label on the bag. Avoid buying seeds from roadsides or markets where quality cannot be guaranteed.
response_tw: T…î aba a w…îakyer…õw w…în fi: agro-input ade…õt…îfo a MOFA agyei, agricultural supply ade…õt…î w…î district titirimpon mu, aba kuo a w…îakyer…õw w…în te s…õ SeedCo, Pioneer, ne Yara, cooperative input ade…õt…î, ne CSIR research stations a w…îtaa t…în breeder anaa foundation aba. Hw…õ daa aba kyer…õws…õm krataa a …õw…î bag so no. B…î hw…õ t…î aba fi kwan ho anaa aguabasa a w…întumi nkyer…õ aho h…î.

instruction_en: What is seed priming and how does it improve germination?
instruction_tw: D…õn na seed priming y…õ na …õboa aba pue s…õn?
response_en: Seed priming is soaking seeds in water or a special solution for a specific time before planting to improve germination rate, speed, and uniformity. Methods include: hydropriming (soaking in plain water for 6-12 hours then drying before planting), and salt priming (soaking in dilute salt solution). Primed seeds germinate faster and more uniformly, which helps crops establish better especially in marginal soils or unpredictable rainfall.
response_tw: Seed priming kyer…õ da aba w…î nsuo anaa aduru a w…îakyer…õ ho bere mu ansa na woto mu s…õ …õsiesie aba pue rate, nt…õm, ne p…õ. Anamm…în ho ne: hydropriming (da w…î nsuo foforo mu dap…õn 6-12 na yaw ansa na woto), ne salt priming (da w…î …õwa a w…îasusu mu). Aba a w…îaprime no pue nt…õm ne p…õ, eyi boa nnuaba si yie titiriw w…î asase a enni pa anaa osu a …õntumi nnim bere mu.

instruction_en: How do I know if seeds are still viable and suitable for planting?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na menim s…õ aba no da sor na …õfata s…õ mede to mu?
response_en: Test seed viability by: placing 10 seeds on a damp paper towel, fold and keep in a warm place, check after 7-10 days. Count how many germinated ‚Äî if 8-10 germinated (80-100%) seeds are excellent; 6-7 (60-70%) are acceptable; below 5 (50%) plant more seeds per hole. Old or poorly stored seeds lose viability ‚Äî check the production date on the bag and do not use seeds more than 2 years old.
response_tw: Hw…õ aba ho pa w…î: de aba 10 si krataa nte so, fa bo mu na guina w…î bae a …õhuru, hw…õ nnafoa 7-10 akyi. Kan a pue ‚Äî s…õ 8-10 apue (80-100%) a aba no y…õ pa pa ara; 6-7 (60-70%) aky…õ; …õsua 5 (50%) a to aba dodo w…î tumtum biara mu. Aba kedee anaa a wonguina w…în yie sua w…în tumi ‚Äî hw…õ y…õ da a …õw…î bag so na mfa aba a w…îadin mfe abien kenkan.

instruction_en: How can children help earn money from farming?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na mmofra tumi ma okuafo adwuma br…õ sika?
response_en: Young people can make farming profitable by: treating farming as a business not a subsistence activity, specializing in high-value crops or livestock products, using technology and innovation, accessing youth-specific agricultural finance programs, connecting to urban markets through digital platforms, forming youth cooperatives for scale and bargaining power, adding value through processing, and combining farming with agribusiness services like input supply or transportation.
response_tw: Mmofra tumi ma okuafo adwuma br…õ sika w…î: hw…õ okuafo adwuma s…õ adwuma na mfa s…õ fie aduan adwuma, paw nnuaba anaa mmoa nnuaba ho adwuma a w…ît…î bo…î k…õse, fa technology ne nnorimde di dwuma, nya mmofra ho okuafo adwuma ka adwuma, ka k…î akura aguabasa w…î digital platforms ho, si mmofra cooperative s…õ fa …îman ne tumi k…õse, de bo…î ka ho w…î y…õ adwuma mu, na ka okuafo adwuma ho nnwuma te s…õ adenne…õ t…în anaa kwan so nnwuma.

instruction_en: What is a farmer field school and how do I join one?
instruction_tw: D…õn na farmer field school y…õ na …õde…õn na meba mu?
response_en: A Farmer Field School (FFS) is a learning program where groups of 25-30 farmers meet regularly in a field to observe, experiment, and learn about agriculture together under a trained facilitator. They learn practical skills by doing rather than just listening. FFS programs in Ghana are run by MOFA, NGOs, and development organizations. Contact your local MOFA extension office or agricultural NGOs in your area to find and join one.
response_tw: Farmer Field School (FFS) y…õ suahu adwuma a okuafo 25-30 kuo hyia p…õ w…î afuo mu s…õ w…îhw…õ, dwiri, ne sua okuafo adwuma bom w…î obi a w…îakyer…õ ase. W…îsua adwuma ho nyansam w…î di dwuma na mfa tie p…õ. FFS adwuma w…î Ghana ne MOFA, NGOs, ne nkosuo kuo di. Ka kyer…õ wo MOFA extension ofisi anaa agricultural NGOs w…î wo beae s…õ wuhu ne ba mu.

instruction_en: How do I access land for farming if I do not own any?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na menya asase ma okuafo adwuma s…õ menni?
response_en: Options for accessing land include: renting from landowners (common in Ghana, negotiate fair terms), sharecropping where you farm and share produce with the landowner, borrowing land from family or community leaders, government allocation programs for young farmers, and cooperative land pooling. Always get written agreements when renting or sharecropping to protect your investment. Contact the Lands Commission or MOFA for guidance on formal land access.
response_tw: Asase nya anamm…în ho ne: as…õ fi asase wura (taa y…õ w…î Ghana, siesie nhy…õso yie), sharecropping a woy…õ okuafo adwuma na fa nnuaba bi br…õ asase wura no, de asase fi abusua anaa oman hene ho, …îman asase br…õ adwuma ma mmofra okuafo, ne cooperative asase ka bom. Kora nhy…õso krataa bere a was…õ anaa sharecrop s…õ …õkora wo sika. Ka kyer…õ Lands Commission anaa MOFA s…õ wunya mmoa w…î asase nya anamm…în mu.

instruction_en: What are the benefits of joining a farmer cooperative?
instruction_tw: Mfaaso b…õn na …õw…î ho w…î si okuafo cooperative?
response_en: Benefits of farmer cooperatives include: collective bargaining power for better input prices and higher selling prices, shared access to expensive equipment like tractors and storage facilities, group loans that may be easier to access than individual loans, collective learning and knowledge sharing, risk pooling among members, access to bulk markets that individual farmers cannot reach, and stronger political voice when advocating for farmer interests with government.
response_tw: Okuafo cooperative mfaaso ho ne: kuo tumi k…õse ma adenne…õ bo…î sua ne t…în bo…î k…õse, ka egyapade a w…îbo…î y…õ den te s…õ tractor ne guina fie ho bom, kuo ka a …õtumi y…õ mmer…õ s…õ nya sen onipa ho ka, kuo suahu ne nyansam ky…õ, mfomso sesae w…î kuo mu, nya aguabasa k…õse a okuafo baako nntumi nk…î h…î, ne kuo mu tumi k…õse bere a w…îka okuafo ho ns…õm kyer…õ …îman.

instruction_en: How do I access international markets for my farm produce?
instruction_tw: ∆êde…õn na meka k…î amanaman aguabasa ma m'afuo nnuaba?
response_en: To access export markets: ensure your produce meets international quality and food safety standards, get certified (organic, GlobalGAP, or Fairtrade if possible), register with Ghana Export Promotion Authority (GEPA), contact exporters and trading companies, join export-focused farmer cooperatives, attend agricultural trade fairs, and use online platforms like TradeAfrica or Alibaba. High-value export crops from Ghana include cocoa, cashew, shea, pineapple, and chili pepper.
response_tw: S…õ wohwehw…õ nya amanaman aguabasa a, kora s…õ wo nnuaba k…î amanaman aho ne aduan ho tumi nhy…õso, nya kratafa (organic, GlobalGAP, anaa Fairtrade s…õ wonya tumi), kyer…õw wo din w…î Ghana Export Promotion Authority (GEPA) h…î, ka exporters ne adwuma kuo ho, ba export okuafo cooperative mu, k…î okuafo adwuma aguabasa, na fa online platforms te s…õ TradeAfrica anaa Alibaba di dwuma. Nnuaba a w…îtaa export fi Ghana ne kookoo, cashew, nkuto, abor…îfo, ne mako hye.

instruction_en: When is the best time to plant maize in Ghana?
instruction_tw: Bere b…õn na …õy…õ papa s…õ wode aburo…î to mu w…î Ghana?
response_en: In southern Ghana, plant maize in March to April for the major season and July to August for the minor season. In northern Ghana, plant from May to June when the rains begin. Planting at the right time ensures the crop gets adequate rainfall during tasseling and silking, which are the most critical growth stages.
response_tw: W…î Ghana apuei fam, de aburo…î to mu w…î ∆Übenem k…î Oforisuo ma akyi ber…õ a na w…î fam k…õse na bere a …õt…î w…î Kutawonsa k…î Oforisuo ma akyiri. W…î Ghana atifi, de to mu fi ∆Üforisuo k…î Ay…õwohomumo bere a nsuo retoe. Bere pa mu to aba mu kyer…õ s…õ afuom b…õnya nsuo a …õy…õ papa w…î bere a aburo…î reto ahuhuruw.

instruction_en: How do I prepare the land before planting maize?
instruction_tw: D…õn na meny…õ asase no so ansa na meto aburo…î?
response_en: Clear the land of weeds and old crop residues. Plough or dig to a depth of 20 to 30 cm to loosen the soil. If possible, apply compost or manure and incorporate it into the soil two weeks before planting. Level the seedbed to ensure even germination. Avoid burning crop residues as this destroys beneficial soil organisms.
response_tw: Yi nhaban foforo ne nnuaba a ato f…õw fi asase no so. Boro asase no kosi s…õntimita 20 k…î 30 mu s…õ asase no atete yie. S…õ wobetumi a, de compost anaa aguare to mu na fa ka ho mpr…õnsa mmienu ansa na wode aba to mu. Siesie seedbed no so yie s…õ aba nyinaa retua f…îw yie. Mma nnuaba fidie anhye efis…õ eyi s…õe asase mu adwummaker pa.

instruction_en: What spacing should I use when planting maize?
instruction_tw: Ahod…î b…õn na mede aba to mu bere a meto aburo…î?
response_en: Use a spacing of 75 cm between rows and 40 cm between plants within the row. Plant 2 seeds per hole at a depth of 3 to 5 cm. After germination, thin to one strong plant per stand. This spacing allows each plant enough space, sunlight and nutrients to grow well.
response_tw: De ahod…î 75 s…õntimita fa ns…õm ho na 40 s…õntimita fa nnuaba ho w…î ns…õm mu. De aba mmienu to mu fa ho biara w…î u…õ 3 k…î 5 mu. Aba tua f…îw akyi, yi nnuaba kosi nnuaba kronkron baako w…î fa biara. Ahod…î yi ma nnuaba biara nya ahorow, owia na ferefere a …õy…õ papa.

instruction_en: What fertilizer should I apply to maize and when?
instruction_tw: Ferefere b…õn na mede to aburo…î ho na bere b…õn?
response_en: Apply NPK 15-15-15 at planting rate of 2 bags per acre mixed into the soil near the seed. At 4 to 6 weeks after planting when the maize is knee high, apply urea (top dressing) at 1 bag per acre. Place urea 5 to 7 cm from the stem and cover lightly with soil to prevent loss through evaporation.
response_tw: De NPK 15-15-15 to mu bere a wode aba to mu, bags mmienu biara eka baako mu, na fa ka ho asase a …õb…õn aba no ho. Nnansa 4 k…î 6 akyi a aburo…î aba mu reb…õ k…îsi ntam, de urea (top dressing) to ho, bag baako biara eka baako mu. De urea sie s…õntimita 5 k…î 7 fi aburo…î no ho na kata so kakra asase mu s…õ …õrenyi fi.

instruction_en: How do I identify a fall armyworm attack on my maize?
instruction_tw: D…õn na …õkyer…õ s…õ fall armyworm atu mako w…î me aburo…î afuom?
response_en: Look for ragged holes in leaves with fine sawdust-like droppings inside the funnel of the plant. The worm itself is greenish-brown with stripes along the body and a distinctive Y-shaped marking on the head. Affected plants will have leaves with irregular feeding damage. Check your field early morning when the worms are most active on the surface.
response_tw: Hw…õ afifide a w…îatwa mu ne aky…õky…õ…õ teketeke a …õte s…õ sawdust w…î nnuaba funnel mu. Adwummaker no ankasa y…õ green-brown na …õw…î lines w…î ne ho so na …õw…î nsono a …õte s…õ Y w…î ne ti so. Nnuaba a yade…õ aba ho w…î nsono a w…îatwa mu te …õnte. Hw…õ wo afuom an…îpa anaana bere a adwummaker no w…î asase so pefee.

instruction_en: How do I control weeds in my maize farm?
instruction_tw: D…õn na meny…õ nhaban foforo a w…î me aburo…î afuom mu?
response_en: Weed at 2 to 3 weeks and again at 6 to 8 weeks after planting. These two weeding operations are critical because early weeds compete directly with young maize for nutrients and water. You can use hand weeding with a hoe or apply pre-emergence herbicides like atrazine before the maize germinates. Mulching between rows also suppresses weeds.
response_tw: Yi nhaban foforo nnansa mmienu k…î mmi…õnsa akyi na bio w…î nnansa nsia k…î nw…îtwe akyi a wode aba to mu. Adwuma nhaban foforo yi mmienu no ho hia pa ara efis…õ nhaban foforo a …õdi kan no di aburo…î foforo bere a …õw…î ho ferefere ne nsuo ho brŒµ. Wobetumi de nsa ne atia yam yi nhaban foforo anaa de herbicides a w…îfre no atrazine to ho ansa na aburo…î aba atua f…îw. Mulching ns…õm ntam mu nso boa nhaban foforo ho.

instruction_en: When is maize ready to harvest and how do I know?
instruction_tw: Bere b…õn na aburo…î atwa so s…õ w…îb…õ yi na d…õn na …õkyer…õ saa?
response_en: Maize is ready to harvest 90 to 120 days after planting depending on the variety. Signs of maturity include dry brown silks on the cob, a hard black layer at the base of the kernel when you remove it, and husks that have turned dry and papery.
